one_line_takeaway: A broad qualitative survey that gives a usable four-axis threat model and attack/defense taxonomy for Federated Learning security, but offers no original experiments and asserts its 'security outranks privacy' headline without a quantitative basis.
key_finding: The survey's headline conclusion is that security concerns in FL are MORE frequent than privacy concerns, and that the three most significant threats are communication bottlenecks, poisoning attacks, and backdoor attacks. Privacy attacks are rarer but judged higher-stakes. It also argues that the diversity of known attacks currently outpaces the maturity of FL defenses, that traditional defenses (homomorphic encryption, secure multi-party computation) lack scalability for FL while differential privacy scales but degrades accuracy (especially on imbalanced/non-IID data), and it recommends adversarial training, privacy-preserving aggregation, and method combination as future directions.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical/systems paper) that organizes the security and privacy landscape of Federated Learning (FL) into a unified taxonomy. Its own contributions are: (1) a threat-model framework with four orthogonal axes (Security vs. Privacy, Server vs. Client, adversary knowledge white/grey/black-box, Insider vs. Outsider with Sybil and Byzantine sub-types); (2) an 'adversarial attack surface taxonomy' splitting attacks into security-targeting (poisoning, backdoor, Byzantine, Sybil, free-rider) vs. privacy-targeting (feature inference, membership inference, reconstruction, attribute/property inference, quantity estimation) — note the paper is internally inconsistent here, filing free-rider under its 'Attacks on FL's Privacy' section heading while describing it as an untargeted performance-degrading (i.e., security) attack; (3) a categorization of defenses by their locus (server, client, communication channel); and (4) a list of open challenges and future-research directions. The authors' distinguishing claim over prior FL surveys is the systematic joint classification of BOTH attacks and defenses plus their qualitative assessment of which threats are most frequent.
threat_model: The paper does not propose a new threat model of its own; it catalogs the FL threat model as its subject matter. Adversaries are FL participants (clients) or a compromised/curious server. Capability is stratified by knowledge (white-box = full access to model parameters/architecture/state; grey-box = black-box plus some non-public statistics; black-box = near-zero model knowledge). Insiders can modify parameters/model behavior (poisoning, backdoor, Sybil via controlling many fake clients, Byzantine via arbitrary updates blocking convergence); outsiders sniff traffic (MITM, side-channel). Attack goals split into integrity/availability (degrade or hijack the global model) and confidentiality/privacy (infer or reconstruct clients' private training data from shared updates). The paper notes that many potent security attacks presuppose white-box knowledge of the aggregation method, which it concedes makes some scenarios impractical.
limitations: It is a qualitative survey with no original evaluation, so its central quantitative claim (security > privacy in frequency) is unsupported by any reported counting methodology or table. No systematic search/selection protocol is described, limiting reproducibility. Table 2's datasets are merely catalogued as 'available for FL deployment,' not used by the authors, so the paper contains essentially no results of its own. Defense coverage is descriptive (mechanism + one weakness each) without comparative effectiveness data. The prose contains errors that undercut rigor (e.g., a dataset row labeled 'Feminist,' and 'IDD' for IID), and several specific numeric/mechanism claims (e.g., the ~90% MNIST worst-case accuracy figure, PDGAN, CentralDP) are attributed to cited works rather than being the authors' findings.

quantitative_results: Table 2 (catalogued, not measured) — Shakespeare: 16,068 items, 715 clients
Cited (NOT the authors' own result): existing adversarial training gives a model ~90% worst-case accuracy on MNIST
Qualitative headline: security attacks reported as more frequent than privacy attacks; top-3 threats = communication bottlenecks, poisoning, backdoor (no counts provided)
baselines_compared: None — survey paper with no experiments and no baseline comparison. Defenses discussed (PDGAN, robust aggregation via Median / Norm-thresholding / Adaptive Federated Averaging / Game-theory, anomaly detection, Differential Privacy incl. CentralDP / Local DP / f-DP, perturbation methods, adversarial/optimized training, SMPC, Blockchain-based FL) are surveyed from prior work, not benchmarked against each other
